Client: It’s mostly interest and dividends now. I think it’s around fifty-five thousand a year.
Advisor: Okay—interest and dividends. When you look at last year or what you expect this year, what’s the actual annual amount?
Client: The number I have written down is 55550 for the year.
Advisor: Got it. And you’re retired, correct—no wages coming in?
Client: Right, retired.

Client: Right, retired.
Advisor: Okay. Next, spending—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Mortgage or rent, utilities, groceries, insurance, the usual.
Client: I keep it pretty steady—2500 a month, give or take.
Advisor: Perfect. Now on debts—any loans you’re paying monthly? Credit cards you carry a balance on, car loan, anything like that?
Client: No. My monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client: No. My monthly debt payments total is 0.
Advisor: That’s helpful. Let me switch to what you’ve saved and where it sits. Roughly, what do you consider your investable assets—accounts you can draw from, not counting the house?
Client: Altogether, investable assets total 306600.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. I’m going to ask what makes up that total, just so I understand the buckets. Do you have a taxable investment account?
Client: Yes. I have a taxable account on a retirement platform, and it’s 132950.

Client: Yes, that one is 41400.
Advisor: Thanks. And then your home—do you own your primary residence outright, and about what’s it worth?
Client: Yes, it’s my primary residence. It’s held at a bank, and the value is 390900.
Advisor: Understood. And just to confirm on loans—no mortgage balance, no other loans outstanding?
Client: Correct, no loans outstanding.

Advisor: And do you know how long it’s set to run—like a covered-until date?
Client: Yes—covered until 2049-07-01.
Advisor: Okay, Linda—thank you. That gives me a clean starting picture: income, expenses, no debt payments, your accounts, the house, and the long-term care policy. Next time we can talk about how conservative you want to stay and what taxes might look like when you draw from these.
Advisor: Linda, before we wrap today, I want to make sure I captured the home and debt pieces cleanly—those tend to drive a lot of planning.
Advisor: On the house value, what number do you want us to use as the current estimate?

Advisor: Got it. And any dependents you’re financially responsible for—kids, grandkids, anyone like that?

Client: Yes, widowed.
Advisor: Thanks, Linda. That helps with how we think about taxes and benefits.

Advisor: Hi Linda—nice to meet you. I’m glad you came in today. Before we get into numbers, what would you like to make sure we cover in this first meeting?
Client: Thanks. I just want to feel… set, I guess. Since my husband passed, it’s all on me, and I don’t want to make a mistake.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it plain-English and step-by-step. Maybe we start with the basics—income coming in, what it costs you to live each month, then what you’ve got saved and any debts. Sound okay?
Client: Yes, that’s fine.

Advisor: Thanks. And then your home—do you own your primary residence outright, and about what’s it worth?

Advisor: Understood. And just to confirm on loans—no mortgage balance, no other loans outstanding?
Client: Correct, no loans outstanding.
Advisor: Last area for today: protection. Do you have any long-term care coverage in place?
